chunk_summary: This section focuses on outpatient recovery criteria and maximum duration for managing severe acute malnutrition (SAM) in India, emphasizing referral protocols for non-responsive cases within community-based management frameworks.
learning_objectives: Understand outpatient recovery criteria for SAM management in India.
Learn referral protocols for non-responsive SAM cases in outpatient settings.
Explore maximum duration guidelines for outpatient SAM treatment.
Examine community-based approaches to SAM management as outlined by WHO and NITI Aayog.
key_facts: Outpatient recovery criteria for SAM include weight gain and absence of nutritional edema.
Maximum duration for outpatient treatment is typically 8-12 weeks, after which non-responsive cases are referred to inpatient care.
Community-based management of SAM (CMAM) is endorsed by WHO, UNICEF, and NITI Aayog for scalability in India.
The Sphere Handbook outlines minimum standards for humanitarian response, including nutritional care for SAM.
India's CMAM report highlights the importance of early identification and treatment of SAM to reduce mortality rates.
reference_quote: Community-based management of severe acute malnutrition (CMAM) is a proven approach to reduce mortality and improve recovery rates in children under five years of age. (Source: WHO, WFP, UNSCN, UNICEF Joint Statement, 2007)
document_key_topics: Maximum duration for outpatient SAM treatment
